html:
RILY: A Dirty Balance Sheet Stub Turning Into an Asset-Backed Earnings Re-Rate

RILY is still priced like an unresolved fraud/liquidity story. That is only half true now. The company remains messy, controlled, investigated, highly levered on a gross basis, and dependent on marks in a concentrated investment book. But the May 2026 facts are materially better than the old bear case.

BRC Group Holdings, formerly B. Riley Financial, has caught up on filings, regained Nasdaq filing compliance, reported a profitable 2025, filed Q1 2026 on time, redeemed its March 2026 notes, cut debt, and now has a much larger public-market asset cushion than the headline balance sheet suggests. The old thesis that the company would simply run out of time before asset values could be realized is getting weaker.

The key Q1 numbers are hard to ignore. In its May 7 Q1 release, BRC reported $211.3 million of net income available to common shareholders, $6.57 diluted EPS, $34.6 million of Operating Adjusted EBITDA, total debt down to $1.30 billion, and net debt down to $372.4 million from $627.0 million at year-end 2025. That followed a 2025 annual report release showing $299.4 million of full-year net income, $112.1 million of Operating Adjusted EBITDA, and net debt down $436.7 million during 2025.

Do not buy the headline P/E. Q1 earnings were heavily driven by trading gains and investment marks. But also do not dismiss them. RILY is a merchant bank and holding company; investment appreciation is part of the model. The right question is not whether $6.57 of Q1 EPS annualizes. It does not. The right question is what the operating platform is worth after marking the investment book closer to current market value.

That is where the market is stale.

At March 31, the company had $705.1 million of Total Investments and net debt of $372.4 million. Its Babcock & Wilcox position was worth about $403 million at quarter-end and represented 63% of securities and other investments owned, per the Q1 filing summary. BW has since traded to roughly $21.85, per current market data on StockAnalysis, compared with a much lower implied March 31 mark. Depending on the exact share count after sales and reporting dates, that alone likely adds roughly $170 million to $200 million of incremental asset value since quarter-end.

That means spot-adjusted net debt is probably closer to $175 million to $200 million today, not the already-improved $372 million Q1 number. Against a current RILY price of $9.01 and roughly $335 million market cap using the latest 37.13 million share count disclosed in May filings, the market is valuing the operating company at roughly $650 million including preferred stock claims. That is about 4x to 5x a plausible 2026 Operating Adjusted EBITDA range of $140 million to $160 million.

For a damaged holding company, that discount is understandable. For a company whose capital markets business just had its most active capital-raising quarter in five years, whose wealth platform still has $11.9 billion of client assets, and whose communications businesses continue to throw off cash despite managed revenue decline, it is too punitive.

The business is not what older RILY bulls often describe. The simple “dominant retail liquidation franchise” pitch is stale. The current company is a more unusual mix: capital markets, wealth management, communications assets such as Lingo and magicJack, consumer products, and a concentrated investment portfolio led by BW. That complexity is why the market struggles with it. It is also why the stock can stay badly mispriced after the filing crisis clears.

The transition now underway matters. BRC plans to repurchase the approximately 8% minority interest in B. Riley Securities and combine it with B. Riley Wealth by year-end, subject to FINRA and other approvals, as described in its April 20 announcement. If completed, that simplifies the structure and creates one integrated small-cap and middle-market capital solutions platform. In Q1, B. Riley Securities executed or participated in nearly $10 billion of capital raised, filed $8.7 billion of new ATMs, and initiated coverage on 26 companies. That is not a dead broker-dealer.

The bear case still has real teeth. The SEC subpoenas and litigation over Franchise Group/Kahn remain unresolved. The 2025 10-K still had material weaknesses in internal control over financial reporting. Preferred dividends remain suspended, and the April 30 missed payment created a preferred dividend default under the preferred certificates. The company still has 2026 note maturities to address, including September and December notes, although subsequent exchanges after quarter-end reduced the burden further. Dilution from bond-for-equity exchanges is real.

But the direction of travel has changed. A year ago, debt maturity risk and filing delays could plausibly overwhelm asset value. Today, the March 2026 notes have been redeemed, Q1 operating cash flow was positive, net debt has been cut sharply, and the public investment book has appreciated further. The risk is no longer “does this survive the next filing deadline?” It is “what multiple should a tainted but cash-generating, asset-backed financial holdco receive?”

Short interest adds torque. Official short interest was still 4.15 million shares, or 25.43% of float, as of April 30, according to ChartExchange. Retail discussion has moved from bankruptcy fear to squeeze mechanics after Q1, but this is not just a meme setup. The fundamental squeeze is that every asset monetization, on-time filing, debt exchange, or clean regulatory update reduces the probability of zero while the stock still trades near liquidation psychology.

The base case does not require heroic assumptions. It requires BW not to collapse, no broker-dealer crippling SEC action, continued debt reduction, and 2026 Operating Adjusted EBITDA around $150 million. Put a 7x multiple on that, subtract roughly $200 million of spot-adjusted net debt and roughly $140 million for preferred liquidation plus accrued overhang, and the common equity is worth around $700 million to $750 million, or about $19 per share on a diluted share base near 39 million.

The bull case is more interesting. If the BRS/BRW combination is approved, capital markets stay active, BW remains strong or is partially monetized, and 2026 maturities are solved without ugly dilution, net debt can approach zero. At that point the market no longer values RILY as a distressed financing problem. It values it as a weird but profitable small-cap merchant bank with embedded public assets. On $175 million to $190 million of forward operating EBITDA at 8x, plus residual investment optionality, $35-plus is reasonable.

Bear case: $4.50, down 50% from $9.01. BW retraces sharply, the SEC outcome damages the broker-dealer, 2026 notes force further dilutive exchanges, and the preferred overhang keeps common equity boxed in.

Base case: $19, up 111%. RILY continues filing on time, settles or contains regulatory risk, realizes enough asset value to handle maturities, and the stock re-rates to a still-discounted 7x forward operating EBITDA.

Bull case: $36, up 300%. BW and other holdings remain strong, net debt is largely eliminated, BRS/BRW integration works, capital markets earnings normalize at a much higher level, and the market stops treating the common as a liquidation stub.
